items.q: What does natural law theory base morality on?
items.correct: Human nature and reason — there are objective purposes (telos) built into humans which reason can discover, generating universal moral precepts.
items.misconception: Natural law means whatever happens in nature is morally right.
items.random_wrong: Natural law is the same thing as the laws of physics.
items.why: The 'naturalistic fallacy' confusion — students assume natural law endorses lions eating gazelles or natural disasters. Aquinas's theory concerns human nature and rational ends, not all natural events.

items.q: What does utilitarianism say is right action?
items.correct: The action that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number — judged by consequences, not intentions or rules.
items.misconception: Whatever makes you personally happy is the right thing to do.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism says you should always tell the truth.
items.why: Students reduce utilitarianism to egoism. It is impartial — your own happiness counts no more than anyone else's. Personal happiness alone misses the aggregative core.

items.q: What does Kant's categorical imperative require?
items.correct: Acting only on maxims you could will to be universal laws, and treating people as ends in themselves, never merely as means.
items.misconception: Treat others how you would like to be treated.
items.random_wrong: Do whatever your conscience tells you to do.
items.why: The Golden Rule depends on your personal preferences (Kant explicitly rejected it). The categorical imperative tests universalisability — whether the maxim can coherently be a law for everyone.

items.q: Is 'do good and avoid evil' one of Aquinas's five primary precepts?
items.correct: No — it is the synderesis rule, the first principle of practical reason from which the five primary precepts (preserve life, reproduce, educate, live in society, worship God) are derived.
items.misconception: 'Do good and avoid evil' is the first of Aquinas's five primary precepts.
items.random_wrong: 'Do good and avoid evil' is one of the Ten Commandments.
items.why: Synderesis sits above the precepts as the foundation of all practical reasoning. Listing it among the five is one of the most common structural errors in natural law essays.

items.q: Does natural law condemn everything 'unnatural', like medicine or spectacles?
items.correct: No — 'natural' refers to what accords with rational human nature and its proper ends, not to avoiding artificial things; medicine serves the precept of preserving life.
items.misconception: Natural law says anything artificial or man-made, such as medicine or glasses, is morally wrong.
items.random_wrong: Natural law says we should imitate the behaviour of wild animals.
items.why: Aquinas's 'nature' is teleological and rational, not 'whatever occurs without human intervention'. Students who read 'natural' colloquially generate absurd objections the theory never faces.

items.q: Which of the following is NOT a primary precept according to Aquinas?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Maximising pleasure. “Self-preservation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is synderesis in Aquinas's ethics?
items.correct: The disposition to apprehend the first principle 'do good, avoid evil'
items.misconception: The faculty of conscience that errs
items.why: Correct answer: The disposition to apprehend the first principle 'do good, avoid evil'. “The faculty of conscience that errs” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many primary precepts does Aquinas identify?
items.why: The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
